ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general toast entry; no specific code for butter-and-sugar toast variant. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar cereal-based products; no dedicated code for butter-and-sugar toast. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for butter and sugar toast. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one thick slice of butter and sugar toast weighing approximately 120 g, as commonly served at Singaporean and Malaysian kopitiams and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Butter and Sugar Toast (F05020013); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and vitamin C
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05020013 (confidence 0.95, approved primary source). The input record cited GrabFood — a forbidden delivery-platform source — for cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C. These four values have been replaced using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data: white bread (FDC ID 325871), salted butter (FDC ID 173410), and granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655). Saturated fat 8.33 g/100g and trans fat 0.42 g/100g are consistent with USDA butter composition scaled to the estimated recipe proportion (~30 g butter per 100 g composite). Cholesterol 25 mg/100g is consistent with the USDA butter-and-bread composite at that proportion. Vitamin C is confirmed 0.0 mg/100g per USDA SR Legacy data for white bread and butter — neither ingredient contributes vitamin C.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. White bread GI reference value.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for butter-and-sugar toast as a composite dish in any approved GI source. GI estimated at 70 based on the University of Sydney GI Database peer-reviewed reference value for white bread (GI 70, glucose scale; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Butter (fat) may marginally lower the effective GI, but the added granulated sugar topping offsets this effect; a conservative GI of 70 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (70 × 57.84 g carbohydrate per 120 g serving) / 100 = 40.5, where carbohydrate per serving = 48.2 g/100g × 1.20 = 57.84 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 501 mg/100g combined with high added sugar (12.7 g/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g), promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit butter and sugar toast due to its high sodium content (501 mg/100g) approaching the upper borderline threshold, compounded by high added sugar that drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; substituting wholemeal bread, reducing butter quantity, and eliminating added sugar are strongly recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~70) with 48.2 g/100g refined carbohydrate and 12.7 g/100g added sugar on a low-fibre base (1.7 g/100g), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid butter and sugar toast as the combination of refined white bread (GI ~70), 12.7 g/100g added sugar, and only 1.7 g/100g dietary fibre produces rapid and pronounced postprandial blood glucose spikes; switching to wholegrain or high-fibre bread without added sugar and reducing portion size is clinically advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33 g/100g and trans fat 0.42 g/100g from butter, both directly atherogenic, combined with high sodium (501 mg/100g) and high added sugar (12.7 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid butter and sugar toast due to its high saturated fat (8.33 g/100g) and trans fat (0.42 g/100g) from butter, which raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherosclerosis; the high sodium and added sugar further increase cardiovascular risk, and substitution with a plant-based spread on wholegrain bread without added sugar is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.33 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; trans fat 0.42 g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory; refined carbohydrates and added sugar further promote inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid butter and sugar toast as butter-derived saturated fat (8.33 g/100g) and trans fat (0.42 g/100g) are established pro-inflammatory triggers, and the refined white bread with added sugar further amplifies systemic inflammation; substituting extra-virgin olive oil spread on wholegrain bread without added sugar is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter and sugar toast is a popular breakfast item widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Southeast Asian region, commonly served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and casual cafes. It consists of thick-cut white bread that is toasted until golden and crisp, then generously spread with salted butter and sprinkled with white granulated sugar. The result is a warm, crunchy slice with a rich, sweet, and slightly salty flavour profile — the butter melts into the bread creating a soft interior beneath the crisp surface, while the sugar adds a pleasant sweetness and occasional crunch. Per 100 g, this food delivers 422 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 22.64 g fat (of which 8.33 g is saturated fat and 0.42 g is trans fat), 48.2 g carbohydrate, 12.7 g sugar, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 501 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 70 (high), making it a high-glycaemic food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high refined carbohydrate and added sugar load on a low-fibre base; unsuitable for hypertension due to high sodium and sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention; and unsuitable for cardiovascular disease due to high saturated and trans fat from butter. A standard serving is one thick slice (approximately 120 g), providing approximately 506 kcal and 57.8 g carbohydrate per serving.
common_user_queries: What is butter and sugar toast?
How many calories in butter and sugar toast?
Is butter and sugar toast healthy?
Can diabetics eat butter and sugar toast?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact record match (F05020013), an approved primary source. Saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and vitamin C have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (white bread FDC 325871, salted butter FDC 173410, granulated sugar FDC 169655), replacing the forbidden GrabFood source from the input record. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database peer-reviewed white bread reference value (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), which is the closest approved analogous source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish (white bread + butter + sugar) is not separately reported in HPB Foodo record F05020013, and USDA SR Legacy does not provide a direct composite entry for this preparation. These values require laboratory analysis or a dedicated composite food entry to populate accurately. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium is not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this item, and no reliable composite estimate from approved sources was available. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in HPB Foodo record F05020013, and no approved-source composite data was available to populate them reliably. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data (white bread FDC 325871, salted butter FDC 173410); neither ingredient contributes vitamin C. 5. The original input record cited GrabFood as the source for cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C — a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 policy. These values have been re-derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data and are consistent with the HPB primary values. 6. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for butter-and-sugar toast as a composite dish was found in any approved source. 7. Trans fat 0.42 g/100g is consistent with butter composition at the estimated recipe proportion and is clinically significant; documented in health context sections. 8. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — check passed.
